title: Cooking Grease / Oil on Cotton
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; powder preload + Dawn, hot wash AFTER chemistry on white cotton
stainChemistry: Cooking grease and cooking oil are non-polar lipids — they don't mix with water, so regular washing just pushes them around instead of removing them. On cotton, fresh oil sits near the surface and lifts easily; aged oil penetrates the fiber and oxidizes into a darker, harder residue. Cotton has the widest home bench for cooking oil — tolerates Dawn, hot wash AFTER chemistry, 3% peroxide on whites, and bounded chlorine on solid white cotton without elastane / trim. The hard rule: powder preload BEFORE water. Adding water to fresh oil first emulsifies it and spreads it into a wider ring. Powder + Dawn × 1-2 + hot wash + air-dry first time is the reliable sequence.
whyThisWorks: Powder preload (cornstarch / baking soda) absorbs the surface oil before any water touches it. Dawn dish soap is engineered for cooking grease — that's literally what it's marketed for. Generous Dawn pretreat with a soft toothbrush emulsifies the oil so water can rinse it. Hot wash is safe AFTER chemistry on cotton because the oil is gone — heat is finishing-side, not setting-side. AIR-DRY first time prevents any residual oil from polymerizing under dryer heat into a permanent yellow shadow.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): cooking grease and oil are triglyceride lipids; non-polar and water-immiscible. Pro uses POG / petroleum ether for oil + amyl acetate for polymerized residue + NSD + 3% H₂O₂ for residual chromophore on whites. Cotton tolerates the full sequence at high temperature.
defaultAssumption: Cooking spatter on a cotton t-shirt / apron / kitchen towel. Fresh = quick win. Aged + hot-dried = often a yellow shadow; pro is the right call. Solid white cotton without elastane = bounded chlorine bleach option for stubborn cases.

spottingProtocol.technique: Pull bulk oil up before water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the spot with cornstarch or baking soda. Press gently. 20–30 min absorb. Soft-brush off. Most surface oil comes off this way without any water — water-first spreads oil into a wider ring.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot — Dawn is engineered for cooking grease. Soft toothbrush 10 min. Cotton tolerates warm Dawn (warm, not hot). Damp-cloth rinse.

spottingProtocol.agent: Test Oily Feel — Repeat Dawn If Yes
spottingProtocol.instruction: Touch with a clean fingertip. Oily feel = repeat Dawn. Cotton typically needs 1 Dawn pass for fresh oil, 2 for aged.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, hot water (per care label)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hot machine wash per care label. Safe at this point — oil is gone, heat is finishing-side. Cotton tolerates hot up to 200°F. Inspect before dryer.

spottingProtocol.agent: AIR-DRY First Time, Inspect Before Dryer
spottingProtocol.instruction: AIR-DRY first time. Inspect in daylight. Faint yellow shadow = repeat the Dawn pass. NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — locks it into cotton permanently as a yellow shadow.

spottingProtocol.agent: Bounded Chlorine Bleach — Solid White Cotton, Last Resort
spottingProtocol.equipment: Chlorine bleach per label, hot wash
spottingProtocol.instruction: Bounded last resort for stubborn yellow shadow on cotton. ONLY if: (1) care label permits chlorine bleach, (2) garment is SOLID white cotton with no colored trim, (3) NO elastane / spandex / Lycra in collar / cuffs / waistband (chlorine destroys elastane permanently), (4) NO other oxidizer (peroxide, OxiClean) in the same wash. Per label. Skip if any condition uncertain.

steps: 1. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
2. Generous Dawn pretreat, warm, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
3. Repeat Dawn pass if oily feel remains.
4. Hot wash per care label — safe AFTER Dawn.
5. AIR-DRY first time, inspect, repeat Dawn if yellow shadow remains.
6. Bounded chlorine bleach for stubborn yellow on solid white cotton without elastane / trim.
products: Cool to warm water for chemistry, hot for wash
professionalProtocol.temperature: warm for Dawn dwell + hot wash AFTER
warnings: Powder BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — polymerizes into cotton permanently
Chlorine bleach: ONLY with care label permission AND solid white cotton AND no elastane AND no trim AND no other oxidizer in the wash
NEVER mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + OxiClean — toxic fumes
Honest cap: hot-dried weeks-old yellowing sometimes plateaus

homeSolutions.agent: Powder First, Then Dawn — Don't Reverse
homeSolutions.instruction: Cooking grease and oil on cotton are tractable IF you do the powder step first. The recurring trap is grabbing a wet washcloth and dabbing at the oil — water spreads oil into a wider ring before chemistry can grip. Powder absorbs the surface oil before any water touches it. Powder, then Dawn, then hot wash, then air-dry first time. NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — locks it into cotton as a yellow shadow.

homeSolutions.agent: Cornstarch or Baking Soda — 20 to 30 Minutes
homeSolutions.instruction: Cover the oil spot with cornstarch, baking soda, or plain baby powder. Press gently. 20 to 30 minutes. Soft-brush off. Most surface oil comes off this way — water-first spreads it.

Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap directly to the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. Dawn is engineered for cooking grease — that's literally what it does. Cotton tolerates warm water for the Dawn step (helps the oil release). Damp-cloth rinse.

homeSolutions.agent: Test for Oily Feel — Repeat If Yes
homeSolutions.instruction: Touch the spot with a clean fingertip. Still feels oily? Repeat the Dawn step. Aged grease often needs 2 Dawn passes; fresh oil usually clears in one.
Hot machine wash per care label. Safe at this point because the oil is gone — heat is finishing-side, not setting-side. Cotton tolerates hot up to 200°F.

homeSolutions.agent: AIR-DRY First Time, Inspect Before Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: AIR-DRY the first time. Inspect in daylight. Faint yellow shadow remaining? Repeat the Dawn pass before any heat. NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — heat polymerizes the oil into the cotton and the yellow shadow becomes permanent.

homeSolutions.agent: Bounded Chlorine Bleach — Last Resort, Solid White Cotton Only
homeSolutions.instruction: Stubborn yellow shadow on solid white cotton: chlorine bleach is the home nuclear option but it's BOUNDED. ONLY use chlorine if (1) the care label permits, (2) the garment is SOLID white cotton with no colored trim, (3) NO elastane / spandex / Lycra in the collar / cuffs / waistband (chlorine destroys elastane — most modern dress shirts have it), and (4) NO other oxidizer in the same wash. Per label dilution, hot wash. Skip if any condition is uncertain.

steps: 1. Powder BEFORE water. NO hot dryer until cleared.
2. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
3. Generous Dawn pretreat, warm, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
4. Repeat Dawn pass if oily feel remains.
5. Hot wash per care label — safe AFTER Dawn.
6. AIR-DRY first time, inspect, repeat Dawn if yellow shadow remains.
7. Bounded chlorine bleach for stubborn yellow on solid white cotton, no elastane / trim, no oxidizer mix.
products: Cool to warm water for chemistry, hot for wash
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot dryer was used on residual oil for weeks (often permanent yellow shadow). Stop if chlorine bleach already damaged collar elastane (irreversible). Stop after full sequence + bounded chlorine plateau.
warnings: Powder BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — permanent yellow shadow
Chlorine bleach BOUNDED — care label, no elastane, no trim, no oxidizer mix
NEVER mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + OxiClean
Aged hot-dried yellowing sometimes plateaus — pro escalation

neverDo: Never water-first on oil stains — spreads them
Never hot dryer with residual oil — polymerizes into cotton permanently
Never chlorine bleach on cotton with elastane — destroys elastane
Never chlorine bleach in same wash as peroxide / OxiClean — toxic fumes
fiberSensitivities: Solid white cotton (no elastane, no trim): widest bench — Dawn, hot wash, 3% peroxide, bounded chlorine bleach
White cotton with elastane: NO chlorine bleach
Colored cotton: skip chlorine, peroxide hidden test
Cotton blend with polyester: cool only — heat sets oil into polyester even if cotton tolerates it
Tolerates hot wash up to 200°F AFTER chemistry

materialWarnings: Powder before water — water-first spreads oil
Hot wash is finishing-side AFTER chemistry, not setting-side
Hot dryer with residual oil = permanent yellow shadow
Chlorine bleach is BOUNDED — check care label, no elastane, no trim

escalation.when: Hot dryer used on residual oil (often permanent yellow shadow). Aged grease through multiple wash cycles with hot drying. Bounded chlorine bleach plateau on solid white cotton. Cotton blend with polyester (cool-only rules).
escalation.whatToTell: "Cooking grease / oil on [solid white / white-with-trim / white-with-elastane / colored / blend] cotton, age [fresh / hours / weeks / hot-dried for years], home-treated with [cornstarch preload + Dawn × 1-2 + hot wash + air-dry + bounded chlorine if eligible]. [Residual yellow shadow / hot-dried suspected / blend complication]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with solvent spotting capability for set yellow on cotton

contextQuestions: Cooking oil (vegetable / olive / canola), animal fat, butter, or rendered grease?
Solid white cotton, white with trim, white with elastane, or colored?
Fresh, hours old, or weeks/months of hot-dried buildup?
Has fabric softener been used on past washes?
Care label — hot-wash and chlorine-bleach permitted?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Cotton's widest-bench advantage is real — full home toolkit applicable on solid white cotton without elastane. Hot-dried oil is the classic yellow-shadow plateau. Modern dress shirts with elastane in collar / cuffs ban chlorine bleach. Cotton-polyester blends fall back to cool-only protocol because polyester locks oil under heat even when cotton can tolerate it.
